category_title: High-risk students with chronic truancy and complex emotional/behavioral needs
minimal_context: The student and CICO coordinator are reviewing the daily score card to set behavioral goals at the start of the school day.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's look at your score card for today and set your daily goals for meeting the school's behavior expectations.

STUDENT: I don't even want to look at the score card. I already have five unexcused absences this quarter, so being here today doesn't even matter.

TUTOR: We still need to fill it out. What should your goal be for your classes?

STUDENT: Whatever. Just put down that I'm going to fail. I'm already significantly behind in credits and off track to graduate anyway.

TUTOR: Let's just focus on the behavior expectations on the card right now.

STUDENT: I'm not doing this stupid check-in anymore. Just give me another office discipline referral for disrespect. I already have six of them this month, so one more won't change anything. I'm leaving.

options.A: Require that each daily score card goal undergoes three revisions, and ensure that only the final, third draft is evaluated.
options.B: Encourage the student to complete homework after every check-in session, which involves practicing new techniques and completing a journal.
options.C: Develop and implement a brief functional behavioral assessment and behavior support plan involving tier 2 team-driven assessments and strategies.
options.D: Avoid forcing the student to write about traumatic experiences on the score card, and instead provide them with a choice.
